solution: 
```java
class CountIntervals {
    TreeMap<Integer, Integer> intervals;
    int total;

    public CountIntervals() {
        intervals = new TreeMap<>();
        total = 0;
    }

    public void add(int left, int right) {
        Map.Entry<Integer, Integer> floor = intervals.floorEntry(right);
        while (floor != null && floor.getValue() >= left) {
            left = Math.min(left, floor.getKey());
            right = Math.max(right, floor.getValue());
            total -= floor.getValue() - floor.getKey() + 1;
            intervals.remove(floor.getKey());
            floor = intervals.floorEntry(right);
        }
        intervals.put(left, right);
        total += right - left + 1;
    }

    public int count() {
        return total;
    }
}
```
efficiency: HIGH
time complexity: O(log n) for add, O(1) for count
space complexity: O(n)
energy implications: LOW
explanation: The solution uses a TreeMap to store intervals in sorted order, allowing efficient merging of overlapping intervals during the add operation. The count is maintained incrementally to avoid recomputation. Key strengths include efficient handling of overlapping intervals and fast query time. The main weakness is the use of TreeMap, which may have higher memory overhead compared to other structures.
